verdicts.reasoning: The ajax_generate_url() handler at Connect.php:110 requires nonce (check_ajax_referer line 113) and admin capability (current_user_can('install_plugins') line 116). The $key is sanitized via sanitize_text_field(). The output goes through wp_send_json_success() which sets Content-Type: application/json. JSON responses cannot trigger reflected XSS because the browser does not render JSON as HTML.

DBRepair::fix_missing_db_tables() at DBRepair.php:35 requires: (1) is_admin_page() check (line 41), (2) current_user_can() check (line 42), (3) check_admin_referer() nonce verification (line 44). The $redirect_page comes from sanitize_key($_GET['page']) which only allows [a-z0-9_-], fed into get_admin_page_url() which builds an admin URL. The redirect target uses wp_safe_redirect() which validates the URL is local. Completely safe.

Tasks/Meta::update() at Meta.php:262 uses $wpdb->update() which is a WordPress core method that internally parameterizes the query. The $row_id is cast via absint() (line 267), making it always a non-negative integer. The $data array is whitelist-filtered against $column_formats (line 286). The $wpdb->update() method handles escaping internally using the provided $column_formats. This is not a SQL injection. The scanner falsely flagged $wpdb->update() as unsafe -- it is safe when used with format arrays as done here. Additionally, the Meta class is only called internally by task processing code, not directly from user input.

QuickConnect::ajax_init_connect() at QuickConnect.php:57 requires nonce (check_ajax_referer line 60) and admin capability (current_user_can line 63). The wp_remote_post() call at line 120 targets a hardcoded URL: $this->get_marketing_site_url() . '/wp-json/smtp-plugin-connect/v1/start-session'. The destination URL is NOT user-controlled -- it is always https://sendlayer.com/... (const SITE_URL line 29). The user-controlled $connection_id (sanitize_key) and $redirect_url (wp_validate_redirect) are passed as POST body parameters to that fixed endpoint, not as the request URL. This is not SSRF.

Same handler as ssrf-001 (QuickConnect::ajax_init_connect). Requires admin auth + nonce. The error_code is sanitized via sanitize_error_code() and output via wp_send_json_error() which returns JSON (Content-Type: application/json). JSON responses cannot be rendered as HTML by browsers, so no XSS is possible.

Same handler as ssrf-001 (QuickConnect::ajax_init_connect). The session_id comes from a server-to-server response (wp_remote_post to sendlayer.com), not from user input. Even if it were tainted, the output goes via wp_send_json_success() which is JSON with proper Content-Type headers. No XSS.

QuickConnect::handle_ownership_verification_callback() at QuickConnect.php:215 is triggered by the init hook and checks for the wp_mail_smtp_sendlayer_quick_connect_verify GET param. It reads a transient (challenge_secret, set by an admin-only AJAX handler) and computes an HMAC-SHA256 signature. The signature is a 64-char hex string output via wp_send_json() which sets JSON Content-Type. The source is a transient set by the plugin itself (not user input), and the output is a deterministic hex hash. No XSS possible. The scanner flagged this as unauth+no nonce, but the endpoint is intentionally public (server-to-server callback from sendlayer.com) and the HMAC output has no exploitable impact.

QuickConnect::handle_auth_complete() at QuickConnect.php:266 is on admin_init, requires is_admin_page() (line 271), current_user_can() (line 282), and wp_verify_nonce() (line 292). The return_url uses wp_validate_redirect() with esc_url_raw() (line 279), which validates the URL is same-host. The redirect uses wp_safe_redirect() (line 297). Triple-protected: auth + nonce + wp_validate_redirect + wp_safe_redirect. No open redirect.

QuickConnect::handle_auth_complete() requires admin auth + nonce (lines 282, 292). The wp_remote_post at line 318 targets a hardcoded URL ($this->get_marketing_site_url() . '/wp-json/smtp-plugin-connect/v1/connection'). The exchange_code is passed as a body parameter to that fixed URL, not controlling the request destination. Not SSRF.

PepipostAPI/Mailer.php:428 prepare_list_of_emails() is a private method that uses array_filter with an inline closure (not a string callback). The closure calls filter_var($email, FILTER_VALIDATE_EMAIL). There is zero command execution here. The scanner incorrectly flagged array_filter() as RCE because it can accept a string callback, but here it uses an anonymous function. No eval, exec, system, or shell execution anywhere in this flow.

Same function as rce-001. The array_map at line 437 uses an anonymous function (closure) that simply wraps emails in an array. This is pure data transformation, not code execution. The scanner incorrectly treats array_map as an RCE sink, but it only becomes dangerous with a user-controlled string callback (e.g., array_map($_GET['func'], ...)). Here the callback is a hardcoded anonymous function.

SetupWizard::get_oauth_url() at SetupWizard.php:738 requires nonce (check_ajax_referer line 740) and admin capability (current_user_can line 742). Output via wp_send_json_success() which is JSON (Content-Type: application/json). The $mailer value is sanitize_text_field'd. No XSS possible from JSON responses.
SetupWizard::get_connected_data() at SetupWizard.php:781 requires nonce (line 783) and admin capability (line 785). Output via wp_send_json_success() (JSON Content-Type). Same reasoning as xss-005.

SetupWizard::install_plugin() at SetupWizard.php:861 requires nonce (line 863), install_plugins cap (line 866), and activate_plugins cap (line 870). The $slug is validated against a whitelist (line 880: in_array against get_partner_plugins()). Output via wp_send_json_success() (JSON). Triply protected and the slug is whitelisted.
Same handler as xss-007 (install_plugin), different branch (line 991 vs 983). Same protections apply: nonce + capabilities + whitelist + JSON output.

SetupWizard::upgrade_plugin_request() around line 1176 requires nonce (check_ajax_referer line 1176) and install_plugins capability (line 1182). The license_key is sanitize_key(). Output via wp_send_json_success() (JSON). The URL generated by Connect::generate_url() always points to the hardcoded https://upgrade.wpmailsmtp.com domain. No XSS.

SetupWizard::send_feedback() at SetupWizard.php:1301 requires nonce (line 1303) and admin capability (line 1305). The wp_remote_post at line 1315 targets a hardcoded URL ('https://wpmailsmtp.com/wizard-feedback/'). The user input ($feedback via sanitize_textarea_field) is passed as form body data, not controlling the request URL. Not SSRF.

DashboardWidget::timespan_select_html() at DashboardWidget.php:498-499 outputs 'echo absint($option)' where $option comes from a hardcoded array [7, 14, 30] (line 498: foreach([7, 14, 30] as $option)). The value is never user-controlled. Even if it were, absint() guarantees a non-negative integer output, which cannot contain XSS payload characters (<, >, ", etc.).

DebugEvents::get_events_short_details() at DebugEvents.php:254 reads from the database using $wpdb->prepare() with %d placeholders (the $ids are array_map('intval')). The array_map at line 263 uses an anonymous function closure, not a string callback. Creating an Event object from DB results and calling get_short_details() is standard OOP, not code execution. The scanner incorrectly flagged array_map with closure as RCE. Additionally, the calling context requires admin capability (line 103 and 146 have current_user_can checks).

DebugEvents/Table.php:289 uses array_map('sanitize_text_field', $dates). The callback is a hardcoded string 'sanitize_text_field', a WordPress core sanitization function. This is not RCE -- the callback is not user-controlled. The scanner incorrectly treats any array_map call as potentially dangerous, but the first argument (callback) must be user-controlled for RCE, and here it is a literal string.

SettingsTab::process_post() at SettingsTab.php:430 is called from Area.php:1176, which is guarded by nonce verification (check_admin_referer at line 1149) and current_user_can() at line 1152. The redirect uses wp_safe_redirect() which validates the URL is same-host. The _wp_http_referer field is sanitize_text_field'd. WordPress's wp_safe_redirect() prevents open redirects to external domains.

ErrorStats::build_error_key() at ErrorStats.php:156 uses preg_replace('/-{2,}/', '-', $part_message) at line 171. The pattern is a hardcoded literal string without the /e modifier. In modern PHP (7+), preg_replace /e is removed entirely. Even in older PHP, this specific pattern has no /e flag. The scanner incorrectly flagged preg_replace as potential RCE. The function performs string normalization on error messages for internal analytics, with no command execution.

Reports/Emails/Summary.php:354 outputs 'echo intval($width)' where $width comes from get_stats_total_item_width() -- an internal method that computes a numeric layout width. intval() always returns an integer, which cannot contain HTML/JS payload. This is used in email template HTML generation (ob_start/ob_get_clean pattern), not in user-facing reflected output. The value is computed internally, not from user input.

summary: All 23 automated findings in wp-mail-smtp are false positives. The scanner suffers from three systemic issues: (1) treating wp_send_json_success/wp_send_json_error outputs as XSS sinks when JSON Content-Type headers prevent browser HTML rendering; (2) treating array_map/array_filter with hardcoded closures or literal string callbacks as RCE sinks; (3) failing to recognize that absint(), intval(), sanitize_key(), wp_safe_redirect(), and wp_validate_redirect() are effective sanitizers. The plugin demonstrates strong security practices throughout: all AJAX handlers use nonce verification and capability checks, the one wp_ajax_nopriv_ endpoint (Connect::process) uses HMAC-SHA512 token verification with single-use deletion, and the QuickConnect ownership verification callback is an intentional public endpoint that only returns HMAC signatures.
feedback: Scanner recommendations: (1) Do not flag wp_send_json_success/wp_send_json_error/wp_send_json as XSS sinks -- these set Content-Type: application/json, preventing HTML rendering. (2) Only flag array_map/array_filter as RCE when the callback argument is user-controlled (from $_GET/$_POST/$_REQUEST), not when it is a hardcoded closure or literal string. (3) Recognize absint() and intval() as complete sanitizers for XSS -- integer output cannot contain payload characters. (4) Recognize wp_safe_redirect() as a complete mitigation for open redirect. (5) The flow from wp_ajax_nopriv_ to Connect::process() was not flagged, but it is actually the most interesting attack surface -- however it is properly protected by HMAC-SHA512 verification.
